video_title: Why Compounding In Investing Feels Like It's Not Working
transcript: [music] >> Welcome to a special series of the Meb Faber Show on the past, [music] present, future of America. I'm sitting down with some of the most notable historians, thinkers, and investors in US financial [music] history, all tied to my new coffee table book, Investing in America, The Rise of a 250-Year Bull Market, out July 4th. >> Meb Faber is the co-founder and chief investment officer at Cambria Investment Management. Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any [music] of Cambria's sponsors on this podcast. All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinions and do not reflect the opinion of Cambria Investment Management [music] or its affiliates. For more information, visit cambriainvestments.com. >> Meb, I am super excited for this episode today, listeners. This is producer Colby here starting the episode instead of Meb. We are going to talk about Meb's new book, Investing in America, The Rise of a 250-Year Bull Market. Listeners, it's now available on Amazon. It'll be released on July 4th. Meb, do you want to start start off with why this book, why this format, and why now? >> By the way, I think I'm the only person on the planet who has ever released a book on July 4th and on a Saturday. There There has to be no overlap. Every marketing guru, Tim Ferriss, 4-Hour Work Week, like there's no one who's ever decided to do this on a Saturday, but I thought it was symbolic. It's the big 250 birthday, so happy birthday, America. The story for this really goes back to COVID period. And we're all sitting at home. And we didn't have anything to do cuz sports weren't on. They closed the beaches here, on and on. And so, of course, America's pastime is taking risk, gambling. People love to gamble. Vegas is closed. There's nothing to bet on, nothing to watch. And so, people started paying attention to the stock market, which was super volatile and you had so many young folks, which is great, get interested in markets. Like, I love that part of it. But, the sad part is they got led in through the casino doors. And this is a huge bummer for me. Look, many podcast listeners have heard me talk about this over the years. We don't teach personal finance in school, although I just saw a recent note that California is making it mandatory by 2031. So, thumbs up, Cali. A lot of folks come to the marketplace, starting to learn about stocks, but you got a bad teacher, bad professor. We all had those, right? And so, I got really frustrated, didn't quite know what to do, but started to have some some burning ideas in my mind. A couple buddies of ours it kind of pushed us forward. I don't know if they know it. So, I'm going to give them a shout-out here. The first is Jim O'Shaughnessy. So, multiple guests on the show over the years. He really deserves to be on the Mount Rushmore of investors, but also educators. Written many famous books, including the Bible of quant investing, What Works on Wall Street. But, Jim used to talk about this concept of time billionaires. Young people who have this just infinite runway of time ahead of them, and coupling that with some concepts of making a little money, saving a little money, investing it in this just magical runway to compounding wealth and becoming fantastically rich over 25, 50 years. And so, that idea was stuck in my head. And I'm a visual learner. People read my books know they're chock-full of charts, pictures, graphs, tables, all that good stuff. And Morgan Housel, who everyone knows, who's written and sold tens of millions of books at this point. You know, Morgan had a great tweet, one of these one-off tweets, but it was a picture of the stock market back to 1900. And when you out on a logarithmic scale, it just looks like a tiny little squiggle that goes up into the right. If you go into financial advisor offices all over the country, you'll see this chart and it lists all the crisis events. And so I said, let's put this together into a beautiful coffee table book. Let's combine these ideas. Let's zoom in on periods of say a decade when things feel so volatile and crazy in the news flow and the geopolitics and everything going on. And yet every decade will zoom back out and look at the course of how things worked out over not just one century in the US, we do two centuries. So all the way back to the founding of the republic and really when stocks started trading in the US. So we started in 1800. And look at this full period and just show how magical it's been to be a long-term investor despite all the crazy events, wars, pandemics, on and on. So that was the origin story behind it. We finally put pen to paper, started writing it. We're going to hint that this is probably part one in a two-part series with the second part coming out hopefully later this year or next year. And that's the companion to this book, which is a global title. We're really excited about it, happy to get more into it, but that's the little bit that tells how it got going. >> And that chart itself, the stat that gets me as I've helped you on this, I don't know how many times I've checked the book itself and the source documents to make sure that the stat is right cuz I don't want to mess it up, but it just seems like even seeing it a hundred times like it's wrong that a dollar in US stocks in 1800 grows to 4.2 [snorts] million real today and 210 million dollars in nominal terms today. >> And this is the funny thing about compounding and people talk so much about it. There's the classic example, you to to kids to try to get them to understand the concept of compounding and the chessboard, you know, where you start with one grain of rice and say, "How many pieces of grain of rice will be on the chessboard if you double for every piece?" And then it's, you know, it just goes up to all more than all the grains of of rice in not just our world, but the universe by the time you get to the end of it. And so, the thing that people rarely grasp is even though the return is 9%. It's not even a double-digit return over that full period compounded over 200 years. And we should have been even more fantastical and started with like 10 bucks or 100 bucks instead of one, but even then we would say it turns into 2 billion or something, 20 billion. But it's just a long runway. And this reminds me of Bessembinder's paper that we talked about with Hank when he was like, "What are the best performing stocks of the past 100 years?" And what stood out was not that the top stocks were massive performers where they're like you would expect them to be like, "Oh, wow, they did 30% or 50% a year or something." He was like, "No, they actually did kind of the stock stock market-like returns, they just did it for 100 years." And so, that's the whole thesis of this book is that, "Hey, crazy stuff happens." One of my favorite quotes in the book is is referencing George Martin. So, for those who watch Game of Thrones, you know, we're still waiting on his book, but people used to always ask him. They said, "George, how do you come up with all these crazy events, these terrible events in history?" And you know, he would often say, "Well, it's based on history and actually far worse things have happened." And so, the fun part about the book is we all live through our own experience. Most of us have only been investing 10, 20 years, older crowd maybe 30, 40. So, you didn't live through the '70s or the 1930s or 1870s or 1720s, all these other times that were really difficult periods to invest. And so, being able to look at this book say, "Hey, times have been crazy and yet it's been a magical wealth compounder. And And one of my favorites about the book is we interlace it with a lot of quotes, things from like, you know, Getty saying, "Hey, look, the door to the millionaire's club in the US isn't locked." And there's so many like that that I just think are so wonderful throughout the book. I think what do we have? Over 70 charts, pictures, tables. It's a lot. >> Listeners, there's a chart of every decade, but then there's also kind of some sidebars as you go through, and some will be about dividend stocks, other ones will be about bear markets. Which one do you think gets the biggest reaction from people over time? >> That'll be funny to watch because you never know with people. And we started to drip out a few of these on Twitter and other places over time, and I think the ones that elicit sort of a visceral reaction. Like, I mean, particularly recently, there's a lot of these big IPOs coming out, right? And I mean, it is a crazy time in the US stock market. And SpaceX, Anthropic, Open AI, and the other 50 ones that are in the queue. And you look back, there's a chart we posted on Twitter and LinkedIn that had industry weightings in the United States in 1900 and 2025. And, you know, the US 125 years ago was mostly railroad companies, right? You had some banks, industrials, a bunch of others like tobacco, telegraph, right? Like, that's an industry. Like, iron, coal, steel. But, fast forward today, and everyone knows the biggest one's tech. But, railroad was kind of the tech of the day, probably could have called it tech. And so, you know, things change over time, and with all this hand-wringing about AI and what's going on in the world about jobs, displacement. And the nice thing about free market experiment that we've been running here and capitalism in general is it has this creative destruction where the names aren't the same, and so there's another chart that lists the top stocks by decade. You know, globally and in the US and you look back to the periods of the 1980s and you say, "Man, wait, that was a bunch of names that don't exist anymore or I don't recognize Atlantic Richfield or General Electric, Eastman Kodak, right? And then you fast forward a decade, a bunch of Japanese companies, fast forward a decade, you know, it's the the more traditional late '90s high fliers that like Cisco, Microsoft, Intel, on and on. Of course, we all know what it is today, but there's some fun ones that just give you perspective and I don't know how many people are going to sit down and read this cover to cover. I think a lot of people it's too heavy to take to the toilet reads. People, this is like a 6-lb book. It's absolutely like we spared no expense on the cover, on the paper, on printing this in Minnesota, but I think people will flip through it, you know, they'll put it on the couch, they'll put it on the entryway at the financial advisory and And the number one sales kind of use case for this is we we tell financial advisors, and by the way, listeners, if you want to buy bulk copies, it's it's for sale on Amazon, uh but if you want to buy 50 or 100, you can get some pretty big discounts of a third or even up to a half. So, you can email us or we'll put the email in the show notes from Atasca is the website if you just Google it, Investing in America. >> We have a website for the book listeners Investing in America book.com and there's one you can look at some of the charts and info on the book, but there's a a link there to take you to the page to get a bulk discount. >> The use case that I keep thinking about is the financial advisor. The next market downturn we have, big fat bear market, your clients are freaking out, just send them the book and say, "Hey, look, look back through history. There's one particular sidebar that we have and I read this at a speech I gave in Omaha the night before the Berkshire meeting." and I said, you know, young investors or or even people that maybe are my age, they'll talk about how hard it's been this century. You know, you live through the dot com bust and the GFC and all these other events. And I said, just but let's put that into perspective. I was like, picture my grandfather, whose name was Mebane, by the way, who was born in 1900. And then we walk through all the things he lived through in his adult life, childhood and adult life, you know, multiple world wars, pandemics that were far worse than anything we've experienced, the Great Depression, on and on, right? And yet, even during that period, stocks were able to withstand and hit all-time highs over and over. So, I think that's a big use case a lot of people will kind of ha- hey, give me some perspective, let me take the long-term and oh, and so, one of the cool part about the charts is the decade charts, it zooms in and says, "Look what happened this decade." And so, sometimes they're terrible, right? 1930s, whatever. And then it says, "But look at the returns for the next 50 years. Look at the returns for the next 20 years." And it kind of zooms out to a point that says, "Okay, even though this was really terrible, awful, you know, the future, if we hold for long enough, has has been bright." >> I mean, don't take money out of the market cuz Trump went to the Knicks game and it was chaos yesterday. Leave it in for the long run. >> It always feels like it at the time and it always feels terrible and impossible, but there's one really interesting nugget in the book. And it's kind of talking about stocks and bonds and it's making the case, you know, it says, "Hey, look, for these long-term investors, stocks on a yearly basis super volatile, you have these outlier years, both nominal and real, and we compare it to bonds, but we say, but let's look at it at a 20-year time horizon." And to me, this is where it gets super interesting because the average return for 20 years, stocks is so much higher, you know, the average return is double or triple that of bonds. The best periods, consistently better. The worst periods are actually better, too, which will surprise people, but the crazy part is it the kink really happens at about 20 years. At a 20-year time horizon, the rolling returns, stocks are equal or less volatile than bonds. For that to sink in to people, say, "Look, if you actually believe you're a long-term investor, if you actually are going to do it and not get shaken out, and your time horizon is 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, they're actually less volatile than bonds historically, which I feel like is a crazy takeaway. Feels like it doesn't make sense. The math doesn't math, but that's what the long-term history's been. >> So, right now, I just looked it up before, Nvidia, I think, is about $5 market cap. One of the sidebars is the first company to whatever dollar amount, I think, million, 10 million, 100 million, billion. If you had to guess today the first to 10 trillion, are you going to take like the Mag 7 plus SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, or the field? >> We may have already dated ourselves, listeners, because we don't publish these day of. I said to friends many times, I said, "If you told me at the end of the year that SpaceX was a $1 trillion company or a $5 trillion company, I would not be surprised in either scenario. In no scenario would I be surprised. If you told me it was 10, you know, at the end of the year, I would say, 'Okay, that's pretty nutty.' And one of the fun parts about looking through history is you find the booms and bubbles and busts, and we have some reading lists. So, like, we probably bought well over 100 financial history books, incredibly obscure ones. Some of these cost us 100, 200 bucks, right? Almost impossible to find. We had some shipped from Canada and other places. Some are picture books that I'd never heard of. So, it was actually really fun last year or so putting this together cuz we got to see a lot of a lot of pieces of of writing I'd never heard of before. But you read about some of these periods in history that were just bananas, right? You know, people get caught up in doing this dumb speculation over and over through history. They lose all their money. The names are different, the booms are different, the reasons are different, but it's the same human element of just getting caught up in the FOMO. They didn't call it that back then, but they called it some similar not too optimistic more disparaging names. But you have all these pieces of artwork depicting these periods, you know, and how these crafty and evil speculators throughout time. So, anyway, but you do have an appreciation for how things can always get crazier. The big thing that feels different about this is the magnitude. I mean, these are giant companies spending giant amount of money. And so, this is going to be a fun timeline. If I had to pick a horse, SpaceX is up there. Eli Lilly has got a dark horse shot coming out. I don't know. But most likely it's going to be a name we've never heard of. >> Listeners, if you go to the website investinginamericabook.com, you can download you can enter your email and download the resource list of books Meb recommends that are kind of financial history books and some other materials. >> There's so many and it's just they're so fun because it's such a story of human ingenuity, progress. You just get so excited and optimistic about the world we live in. I've joked with you that we wanted to our next our next coffee table book is going to be called everything is getting better and just a chart full of just like the progress of humanity of the last couple hundred years. We put our heart and soul in this book. I think it came out great. It's going to be a lot of fun. We'd love to hear what you think, listeners. So, if you pick up a copy, please leave a review. We like to read them and uh certainly grab some for some friends for Father's Day. We got here 4th of July, all these sort of fun events. I think it's a perfect gift to pass along as well for even your young investors, too. >> And listeners, if you have questions or thoughts, too, you can one, leave leave a the review on Amazon, but you can email us at hello@investing in americabook.com. The book obviously goes through a ton of people throughout. Who is who's I don't know if you could say your favorite, but who would be your favorite if you had to pick one? >> So, some of the early examples, you look at how politicians are just shameless today about trading stocks and launching coins and just the amount of grift that's going on. But, you look back to the time of Jefferson and Jackson and people were doing the same thing. You look at some of these speculators trying to corner the gold market, try to insider trade bonds, all these just various periods throughout history, and it's just kind of a constant theme of evolution of regulators versus the scoundrels, right? So, there's a whole host of those. I mean, Buffett certainly gets his references throughout, the most famous retiree in the country now, but he's arguably been the goat of doing this and the the compounding stats that we talk about with him as well. So, there's quite a few pretty interesting characters throughout. >> Good no answer. I was going to guess you were going to say Hetty Green. I think she was Mark Higgins' favorite, too, but I feel like I've heard her talking about you. >> She's great. Particularly in a time where you go back 100 years and look, one of the tongue-in-cheek things about this book is 100 years ago, what, like a couple percent of the population invested in stocks? Well, I should say 125, 150 years ago. 1920s, it really ramped up. Then you have people like Hetty Green, who's also a female, so that was even more of a rarity, and just a total baller trader. She was incredibly smart, incredibly tough. She's a super fun one to read about. >> So, zooming in to today, we're at a point where there's a lot of concern about debt to GDP in the US. Your favorite cape is almost taking out our prior high and stocks are very expensive. Country's very polarized. AI is possibly one of these new innovations that could go down in the history books as one of uh the most impactful ever. Is this like different than the past or is this just the same as it's been in other decades and zoom out? >> I thought about putting in a postscript and just titling it something like, "Well, you know, maybe not right this second, but it actually it hits upon exactly what we're talking about, which is even if you bought stocks in '99, right? Fast forward 25 years, you've done okay, right? Like you've done just fine if you bought at the absolute peak. We talked about this with Ben Carlson on the podcast recently about old Bob, the world's worst market timer. So, even if, you know, stocks get knocked down 50 from here, chances are 20, 30, 50 years from now, they'll be totally okay. Maybe not the next 1 3 5 10. And maybe that's a good lead in to our next book on global investing and why that's a little more thoughtful than just one country. But over time, that's the whole thesis of the book is that when you zoom out, valuation matters less than maybe what it matters in the short term. And who knows, Colby, we could have an escalation of valuations go on up and challenge our old favorite example of Japan. I never thought we'd see it in the US again like the late '90s, but we got the granddaddy of them all, Japan, lurking here, the Mount Everest of valuation bubbles, but that's probably a double from here. So, uh bring it on, SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic. We'll see what y'all got. >> So, before we wrap up, listeners, again, you can buy the book on Amazon, go to the website investinginamericabook.com. You can download resources. You can shoot us an email with any questions. We normally get some questions in the feedback at the mebfabershow.com. We're not going to do any real investing ones, but we did get one related to this book. So, Meb, I'll just let you answer this, and I think this is a good way to make sure that people I preview the question, it's a good one to make sure they don't get the wrong uh lesson from the book. >> Hi, Dad. It's me, Anton. Congratulations on your book coming out. I have a question for you since you seem to like finance. If I had $100 to invest today, what should I do with it? >> That's amazing. He There's no chance he knows what finance is. >> He's his his mother's son. >> Well-spoken though. You know, it's funny cuz when we talked to him, we made this joke before, I think, on the podcast and Twitter, and I said, "When my wife's book, The Department, came out, listeners, pick up a copy, and you can have You can have a dual two-book order. That one's a little spicier fiction though. You know, Jackie's book, we were talking, and and I said, "You know, son, by the way, you know, Dad's an author, too." And he's like, "Really?" I said, "Yeah, you know, I wrote five books before you were born." I said, "Now, I've written zero since you've been born. Now, that's worth it. You've been great, and a good use of my time, and I love hanging out with you, but you're nine, you can take care of yourself today." Well, the question was, "What would I invest 100 bucks in?" >> Yep. >> Anton, you don't have 100 bucks, first of all. Second of all, if you did have 100 bucks, I like the idea of our old podcast alum, Griffin, on kind of what they do. It feels very tangible to me. Anton's going to have a Invest America account come this summer. He sadly is too old for the $1,000 grant, but we'll put a little in retirement account. I don't love the kind of stock picking ideas, but making it tangible to a real world company and say, "Hey, look. Each month we'll put $10 in a company that you experience in the real world and you love. You know, and we'll talk about why Five Guys may or may not be available to invest in, and but maybe it's McDonald's, and we can talk about Nike and why maybe it's gone down so much over the past number of years and show you that you can actually lose money, too. And so maybe just pick one every month for 10 bucks. I think that'd be a fun way to do it. Griffin does it automatically, so I think that's a little less fun. Hey, I even let you make the trades. Maybe that's an inspiration for our next book, which is, you know, how to teach your children to invest and and kind of walk through a timeline and a recipe book for the best way to do it and the best way not to do it. I think a way is a lot of the traditional parents do, which is to kind of let their kids trade an account, and if and when they invariably lose all the money, it creates a lot of emotions of shame. They don't want to talk about it. But having the experience where like these are lessons learned, and I've actually thought about writing a book like this where each chapter is like, "Hey, you know, it's actually like short a stock. Hey, try to invest in research some mutual funds." And then on and on. Anyway, thanks Anton. Good question. We'll we'll put it to work. >> You just earned yourself $10 a month in stock going forward, smart kid. >> Yeah. >> All right, listeners, thanks for listening. Go check out the book Investing in America, and shoot us your feedback, and thank you. >> Happy birthday, America. >> Podcast listeners, we'll post show notes to today's conversation at mebfaber.com/podcast. If you love the show, if you hate it, shoot us feedback at themeebfabershow.com. We love to read the reviews. Please review us on iTunes and subscribe the show anywhere good podcasts are found. Thanks for listening, friends, and good investing. >> Yay!
